coredata.dc:description: This study focuses on synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) integrated CeO2:3Tb3+ nanocomposites (CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs) for the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye. The NCs are synthesized via a hydrothermal method using coffee grounds as the CD source and characterized using XRD, FTIR, Raman, TEM, XPS, UV-Vis, and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs exhibited a dramatic 29.2-fold enhancement in PL intensity compared to the CeO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles alone, attributed to the Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) mechanism with 75 % efficiency. With an emission peak at 543 nm, the NCs achieved excellent color purity (98 %) and a correlated color temperature (CCT) of 4937 K, making them suitable for cool LED applications. In photocatalytic testing, CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs demonstrated superior degradation efficiency, achieving 98 % removal of MB dye, outperforming individual CeO2 and CeO2:Tb3+ components. To assess environmental safety, the treated water is used in a seed germination test with chickpea seeds (Cicer arietinum). The CTC-4 treated groups significantly enhanced seedling growth, with shoot and root lengths of 4.13 ± 1.21 cm and 2.82 ± 1.08 cm, and diameters of 2.72 ± 1.04 mm and 1.67 ± 0.52 mm by Day 7. Root branching reached 20 compared to 17 in the control. Phytotoxicity assessments showed MB dye inhibited germination (PIG 100), while CDs(4 wt%)@CeO₂:3Tb3+ showed negligible toxicity (PIG −0.2). The CTC-4 group achieved the highest Vigor Index I (695) and germination index (129.9), with a shorter germination time of 24.0 days. These results demonstrate the potential of CDs(4 wt%)@CeO₂:3Tb3+ NCs for mitigating MB dye toxicity and promoting healthy seed growth in dye-contaminated environments. These findings indicate that the CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs are highly effective, eco-friendly photocatalysts for dye detoxification in wastewater treatment, with potential applications in sustainable agriculture and environmental management. This study establishes the CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs as a promising solution for industrial dye pollution, offering both environmental and agricultural benefits.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study focuses on synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) integrated CeO2:3Tb3+ nanocomposites (CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs) for the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye. The NCs are synthesized via a hydrothermal method using coffee grounds as the CD source and characterized using XRD, FTIR, Raman, TEM, XPS, UV-Vis, and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs exhibited a dramatic 29.2-fold enhancement in PL intensity compared to the CeO2:Tb3+ nanoparticles alone, attributed to the Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) mechanism with 75 % efficiency. With an emission peak at 543 nm, the NCs achieved excellent color purity (98 %) and a correlated color temperature (CCT) of 4937 K, making them suitable for cool LED applications. In photocatalytic testing, CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs demonstrated superior degradation efficiency, achieving 98 % removal of MB dye, outperforming individual CeO2 and CeO2:Tb3+ components. To assess environmental safety, the treated water is used in a seed germination test with chickpea seeds (Cicer arietinum). The CTC-4 treated groups significantly enhanced seedling growth, with shoot and root lengths of 4.13 ± 1.21 cm and 2.82 ± 1.08 cm, and diameters of 2.72 ± 1.04 mm and 1.67 ± 0.52 mm by Day 7. Root branching reached 20 compared to 17 in the control. Phytotoxicity assessments showed MB dye inhibited germination (PIG 100), while CDs(4 wt%)@CeO₂:3Tb3+ showed negligible toxicity (PIG −0.2). The CTC-4 group achieved the highest Vigor Index I (695) and germination index (129.9), with a shorter germination time of 24.0 days. These results demonstrate the potential of CDs(4 wt%)@CeO₂:3Tb3+ NCs for mitigating MB dye toxicity and promoting healthy seed growth in dye-contaminated environments. These findings indicate that the CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs are highly effective, eco-friendly photocatalysts for dye detoxification in wastewater treatment, with potential applications in sustainable agriculture and environmental management. This study establishes the CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs as a promising solution for industrial dye pollution, offering both environmental and agricultural benefits.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: MB is a heterocyclic aromatic compound widely used across various industries, including textiles, cosmetics, bio-pigments, pulp, and paper production, due to its affordability, water solubility, and excellent color properties [1,2]. However, MB effluents are known to be ecologically hazardous, carcinogenic, and possess a foul odor, raising significant concerns about environmental and health risks. Exposure to MB-contaminated water can adversely affect human health, potentially causing harm to the nervous system, eyes, and respiratory system, as well as inducing nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and gastritis [3]. Moreover, MB in aquatic environments can obstruct sunlight penetration and reduce oxygen levels, impairing the functionality of aquatic ecosystems and posing a significant threat to marine life [4]. Consequently, developing effective strategies for removing such pollutants from water sources is of utmost importance. Among the various approaches, photocatalytic degradation has gained significant attention due to its eco-friendliness, energy efficiency, and sustainability [5,6]. This technique is particularly attractive as it degrades MB into carbon dioxide and hydrogen without generating secondary pollutants, making it a cleaner and safer method for environmental management [7].

The use of photocatalytic degradation powered by solar energy is widely considered one of the most promising methods for eliminating organic pollutants due to its high efficiency, sustainability, low toxicity, and cost-effectiveness [8]. Recently, semiconductor (SC)-based photocatalysts have gained popularity in addressing environmental issues, particularly in water pollution control [9]. These SC photocatalysts are valued not only for their capacity to degrade dyes but also for their ability to modify redox potentials, which enhances dye degradation in contaminated water. Among the established SC photocatalysts, cerium oxide (CeO2) in its cubic phase has demonstrated potential for breaking down various organic pollutants under UV light, owing to its physicochemical stability, non-toxicity, affordability, and photocatalytic activity comparable to renowned catalysts like TiO2 [10]. Furthermore, CeO2 exhibits appropriate redox potentials, promoting the generation of reactive intermediates such as hydroxyl (OH•) and superoxide (•O2 -) radicals, both critical for dye degradation. Despite these promising attributes, the efficiency of CeO2 is often limited by its wide band gap energy (∼3 eV) and the rapid recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. To overcome these challenges, doping CeO2 with various metal and non-metal ions has been investigated to optimize its optical band gap, morphology, surface area, and oxygen vacancies, thereby improving its photocatalytic performance [11,12].

Recent studies have highlighted the application of terbium oxide (Tb2O3), a multivalent lanthanide oxide, in constructing photocatalysts [13–16]. The self-redox center of Tb4+­/Tb3+ in Tb2O3 is reported to facilitate electron-hole separation and enhance the production of hydroxyl radicals, thereby improving the photocatalytic efficiency of semiconductors [17]. It is suggested that Tb2O3 loaded on the surface of Ag2Mo2O₇ can promote photogenerated electron migration and facilitate the separation of photoexcited carriers [18]. Moreover, the Tb4+­/Tb3+ redox cycle in Tb2O3 can generate hydroxyl radicals, which play a critical role in photodegrading stable organic pollutants. Thus, doping CeO2 with Tb offers a promising strategy to enhance photocatalytic performance. Furthermore, CDs with sizes below 10 nm have been combined with other photocatalysts to enhance photocatalytic performance, as they extend visible light absorption and exhibit excellent electron transfer and reservoir properties. Various CD-based photocatalysts, such as CDs/C3N4 and CDs/BiVO4/CdS, have been reported as effective solutions to improve photocatalytic efficiency [19,20]. A summary of the photocatalytic properties of various CeO2-based hybrids for dye degradation is presented in Table 1 [21–29]. Based on literature observations, combining CDs with CeO2:Tb3+ can synergistically enhance photocatalytic efficiency, overcoming the limitations of each material individually. Consequently, this study developed a novel nanocomposite (NC) using CDs and CeO2:Tb3+ to evaluate its photocatalytic performance in degrading MB dye.

Methylene blue is widely used as an aniline dye for coloring various manufactured products, particularly wool, silk, and cotton, and is often found in textile wastewater effluents. It poses significant health risks to humans, animals, and even plant life. Consequently, the removal of MB from wastewater is a high priority for industrial societies. Water contamination has emerged as a critical concern, threatening agricultural lands and their yields. Untreated industrial effluents, especially those containing dyes from industries like paint, leather, and textiles, can severely impact soil quality and plant health [30]. Given that MB-contaminated water is often used for irrigation in developing countries, there is an urgent need to develop efficient photocatalysts for detoxifying dye molecules in wastewater [31]. Therefore, this study also examines the impact of dye-degraded water on seed germination.

In this work, CeO2:Tb3+ is selected as the host-dopant system due to the remarkable optical and structural stability of CeO2, which makes it an ideal candidate for a variety of photonic and photocatalytic applications. The incorporation of Tb3+ ions in the CeO2 matrix offers the advantage of efficient green emission (around 545 nm) through f–f transitions, making it suitable for applications in lighting, display devices, and sensors. CeO2 itself is known for its high chemical stability, non-toxicity, and ease of functionalization, all of which are beneficial in photocatalytic processes. A doping concentration of 3 mol% Tb3+ is carefully chosen and optimized based on prior studies, which suggest that this level of doping results in the most favourable luminescence properties. At this concentration, Tb3+ ions provide strong emission without the detrimental effects of concentration quenching, which typically occurs when the dopant ions are present in high concentrations, leading to non-radiative recombination pathways and reduced fluorescence efficiency.

In addition to the CeO2:Tb3+ host-dopant system, CDs derived from waste coffee powder are introduced at a concentration of 4 wt%. This sustainable approach to synthesizing CDs not only addresses environmental concerns but also enhances the photocatalytic performance of the nanocomposite. The CDs, due to their small size, high surface area, and unique optical properties, significantly enhance the visible-light absorption of the composite material. This leads to improved charge separation and efficient electron transfer processes, which are essential for photocatalytic reactions under visible light. The resultant CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs exhibit synergistic photocatalytic and optical properties, combining the benefits of both CeO2:Tb3+ phosphors and the CDs for enhanced photocatalytic efficiency, making them highly promising for energy and environmental applications.
( Chart 1).

The CeO2:Tb3+ solid solutions (0–5 mol%) are synthesized using a hydrothermal method. Appropriate quantities of Ce(NO3)3 and Tb(NO3)3 are individually dissolved in water, mixed, and co-precipitated using NH4OH, maintaining a pH ∼10. The resulting gels are placed in Teflon-lined steel autoclaves and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 260 °C for 10 h ( Fig. 1a). After treatment, the autoclaves are cooled, and the crystallized powder products are thoroughly washed with deionized water and dried at room temperature

CDs are synthesized from coffee ground waste through a hydrothermal treatment process. Nitric acid (70 %, Ducksan Chemical) is used as the oxidizing agent, while Fe(NO3)3 acted as the catalyst. The synthesis involved mixing 1 g of coffee ground waste, 0.76 g of iron nitrate, and 1 mL of nitric acid with 40 mL of deionized water. The mixture is stirred vigorously for 10 min before being transferred into a Teflon vessel placed inside a stainless-steel autoclave. The hydrothermal treatment is conducted at 200 °C for 20 h. The reaction led to a color change from transparent to yellow, signifying the formation of CDs. The resulting solution is then purified by membrane filtration, followed by dialysis against deionized water, as depicted in Fig. 1b.

To prepare the CDs@CeO2:Tb3+ NCs, 0.5 g of CeO2:Tb3+ NPs are mixed with CDs in varying mass ratios (1, 2, 4, and 7 wt%) in ethanol (1 mL), and the pH is adjusted to 6.5 using hydrochloric acid. The mixture underwent ultrasonication at room temperature until a uniform gel is formed. The gel is then dried under vacuum at 100 °C for 14 h, and the resulting xerogel is ground into a fine powder to obtain the final CDs@CeO2:Tb3+ NCs.

The photocatalytic efficiency of the synthesized catalyst is assessed by evaluating the photodegradation of MB using a batch method. A 1 g/L stock solution of MB is prepared, from which a standard 100 mg/L solution is created. The photodegradation experiments are performed under UV irradiation using six mercury lamps, each with a power of 11 W and emitting light at an intensity of 50 lx at 260 nm. Before initiating the photocatalytic experiments, the mixtures are stirred in the dark for 30 min to reach absorption-desorption equilibrium. Several key parameters influencing the photodegradation of MB dye, such as solution pH, catalyst dosage, irradiation time, and the initial MB concentration, are systematically studied to determine the optimal conditions for maximum degradation. The concentration of the residual MB dye is monitored by measuring its absorbance at 665 nm using a Jenway 6800 UV/VIS spectrophotometer.

The impact of the photocatalytic treated water on chickpea (Cicer arietinum) seed germination is evaluated following the method described in [32]. For each test, 5 sets of seeds are presoaked in the treated aqueous samples for 24 h before being transferred to sterilized filter papers arranged in Petri dishes. The seeds are then kept in the dark at 28 ± 3 °C. The germination time and percentage of germinated seeds in each Petri dish are recorded. After 7 days, the average shoot and root lengths of the seedlings are measured. Each treatment is conducted in triplicate, and the results are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The germination percentage is calculated by dividing the number of germinated seeds by the total number of seeds sown and multiplying by 100.

The structural properties of the samples are analyzed using various characterization techniques. X-ray diffraction (XRD) is performed with a Shimadzu 7000 powder X-ray diffractometer (CuKα radiation, λ = 1.5406 Å). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra are recorded in the 500–4000 cm−1 range using a Nicolet FT-IR-400 instrument. The morphology and elemental composition of the samples are examined using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM-7000F) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM-2010). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis is carried out using a Vacuum Generator (VG) ESCALABMKII spectrometer with an AlK source, and the C1s peak (284.6 eV) is used for calibrating the peak positions of insulating samples. Ultraviolet–visible (UV/Vis) absorbance is measured using a Shimadzu/UV 18000 spectrometer. Raman shifts are analyzed using a Raman spectrometer (LabRam-HR 800, Horiba Jobin-Yvon), while PL measurements are conducted with a Horiba spectrofluorometer.

The XRD patterns for CeO2:Tb3+ (0–5 mol%) are illustrated in Fig. 2a. The identified lattice planes (111), (200), (020), (311), (222), (400), (331) and (422) consistent with ICDD#00–034–0394, confirm that all synthesized nanoparticles exhibit a cubic fluorite structure with the space group Fm-3m characteristic of CeO2, without any detectable impurity phases [33–35]. This indicates that the Tb3+ dopants are uniformly incorporated within the ceria matrix, ensuring a homogeneous distribution of dopant cations throughout the system. The retention of the ceria crystal structure upon Tb3+ doping suggests that the introduction of Tb3+ ions does not significantly disrupt or alter the existing lattice, showcasing the structural stability and robustness of CeO2 even after modification with Tb3+ cations. As seen in Fig. 2a1, an increase in Tb3+ concentration results in a shift of the (111) plane peak towards a lower angle. This shift occurs because Tb3+ ions, upon substituting Ce4+­ ions, cause potential lattice contraction, given their smaller ionic radius. However, the presence of Ce3+ ions with a larger ionic radius in the nano-lattice, which can also be replaced by Tb3+, helps maintain the lattice parameters. Charge neutrality is achieved by forming oxygen vacancies (Vₒ) or reducing some Ce4+­ ions to Ce3+, which balances the charge difference between Tb3+ (+3) and Ce4+­ (+4). These oxygen vacancies play a crucial role in influencing the functional properties and structural behaviour of CeO2, affecting conductivity and defect concentration [36].

The XRD pattern of CDs in Fig. 2b shows broad peaks centered at 2θ = 20° and 40°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of amorphous carbon. Moreover, the XRD pattern of the CDs@CeO2:Tb3+ NCs displays characteristic diffraction peaks of CeO2:Tb3+, confirming the incorporation of CDs into the CeO2:Tb3+ matrix [37]. The structural parameters of CeO2:Tb3+ NPs are further analyzed using Rietveld refinement with the FullProf suite, as shown in Figs. 2c and 2d, for CeO2 and CeO2:3Tb3+, respectively. A background fit is refined in tandem with other structural parameters, and peak shapes are analyzed using a Pseudo-Voigt function (a linear combination of Gaussian and Lorentzian). The refined parameters are then used to create a Crystallographic Information File (CIF), which is visualized using the VESTA program [38]. The refined structure of the CeO2 system confirmed the absence of any impurity phases. The CeO2 crystal structure contains three atoms per asymmetric unit, with Ce4+ cations occupying the Wyckoff (4a) sites at (0, 0, 0) and oxygen anions at the Wyckoff (8c) sites at (1/4, 1/4, 1/4) [39]. The low dislocation density values indicate high crystallinity of the synthesized nanoparticles, as summarized in Table S2.

FT-IR spectroscopy is employed to identify functional groups, analyze molecular vibrations, and detect metal-oxide stretching vibrations, particularly the presence of OH radicals, which play a vital role in photocatalytic mechanisms [44]. The FT-IR spectra of CeO2, CeO2:3Tb3+, CDs and CDs (4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs are depicted in Fig. 3a, with the corresponding functional groups and associated bands listed in Table S3. The presence of identical bands in both CDs and CeO2, as well as in the NCs, confirms the successful incorporation of CDs into the CeO2:3Tb3+ matrix. Additionally, the observed hydroxyl groups (–OH) on the surface of the samples play a pivotal role in enhancing photocatalytic activity through various mechanisms [45–47].

Raman spectroscopy is a powerful tool to identify oxygen sub-lattice distortions and detect the presence of oxygen vacancies (Vₒ) in ceria-based materials. The Raman spectra for the series of Tb and CDs containing CeO2 NPs are presented in Fig. 3b. Two prominent features are observed: one at a lower wavenumber of 450 cm−1 and another at a higher wavenumber around 585 cm−1, as summarized in Table S4. The fluorite structure of CeO2 is characterized by a significant peak at 450 cm−1, which corresponds to the F2 g vibrational mode. This peak represents the symmetric stretching of eight oxygen atoms surrounding a cerium ion. The typical Raman spectrum of CeO2 exhibits this characteristic F
2 g peak at 450 cm−1, along with a broader feature centered around 585 cm−1, which is associated with oxygen vacancies (Vₒ). These observations are consistent with previous reports on CeO2 nanoparticles [47,48]. Introducing Tb3+ into the CeO2 matrix leads to a decrease in the intensity of the main F2 g band and enhances the intensity of the 585 cm−1 band. This reduction in peak intensity with increasing Tb3+ concentration may be attributed to the phonon confinement effect, particle size reduction, or an increase in oxygen vacancy concentration, aligning well with the results obtained from XRD analysis [49]. The Raman spectra of CDs display two notable bands: the D band (∼1338 cm−1), indicating the presence of amorphous carbon, and the G band (∼1590 cm−1), related to sp²-hybridized graphitic carbon structures [50]. For the CDs (4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs, the bands characteristic of both CDs and CeO2 nanoparticles are present, confirming the successful incorporation of CDs into the CeO2 structure. The ratio of the oxygen vacancy signal (AVₒ) to the F2 g signal (AF2g) provides insight into the concentration of Vₒ in the NCs [51]. Table 5a shows the AVₒ/AF2g values for all synthesized NCs, indicating that the concentration of Vₒ increases with higher Tb3+ doping, which is consistent with findings from other analyses. The substitution of Tb3+ ions, having a larger ionic radius compared to Ce4+­, introduces lattice strain and defects, which are reflected in the Raman spectra. Notably, the CDs (4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ sample exhibits a significant increase in the AVₒ/AF2g value, suggesting an enhanced concentration of Vₒ, which plays a crucial role in improving photocatalytic efficiency [52–54]. Furthermore, the intensity ratio of the D to G bands (I
D /I
G ) offers valuable information about the structural disorder and graphitization degree in CDs. A higher I
D /I
G ratio suggests more defects and disorder in the carbon structure, while a lower ratio indicates a more ordered and graphitic structure. As seen in Table 5 b, the slight increase in the I
D /I
G ratio suggests an increase in defects, which positively influences the photocatalytic activity.

The UV–Vis spectra of the samples CeO2 (C), CeO2:3Tb3+ (CT), and the NCs with varying CDs content: CDs(1 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ (CTC-1), CDs(2 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ (CTC-2), CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ (CTC-4), and CDs(7 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ (CTC-7) are shown in Fig. 3 c. The absorption spectra, recorded within the 250–800 nm range, exhibit strong absorbance near 300 nm, indicative of UV-range absorption. These peaks are attributed to charge transfer from the 2p state of O²- in the valence band (VB) to the 4 f state of Ce4+­ in the conduction band (CB). This transfer results in direct recombination between electrons in the Ce4+ (4 f) CB and holes in the O²- (2p) VB [55]. The optical band gap energy (E
g ) of the samples is calculated using Tauc's relationship [56]:
(7)
α h ν = A
h ν −
E
g
n
where hν is Planck's constant, A is the proportionality constant, ν is the photon frequency, α is the absorption coefficient, E
g is the energy band gap, and n indicates the type of electronic transition.
Table 2 lists the calculated Eg values for all samples. Fig. 3d presents the Tauc’s plot [(αhν)² versus photon energy (hν)] for C, CT, CTC-1, CTC-2, CTC-4, and CTC-7 NCs. The undoped CeO2 nanoparticles exhibit a direct band gap energy of 3.23 eV, which gradually increases with Tb doping, as shown in Table 2. This band gap widening, accompanied by a blue shift in the absorption spectra, is attributed to the transition from Ce4+ to Ce3+, resulting in a broader charge-transfer gap between the O 2p and Ce 4 f bands. The blue shift also corresponds to reduced Ce3+ concentration and fewer oxygen vacancies, which contribute to band gap widening [57]. The incorporation of CDs into CeO2:3Tb3+ alters the band gap in the UV spectra by introducing surface states or defect levels, which narrow the band gap. CDs typically enhance light absorption in the visible region, extending the photocatalytic response beyond the UV range. They also facilitate charge transfer between CeO2 and CDs, reducing electron-hole recombination and effectively modifying the energy levels. This leads to a redshift in the absorption spectra and a lowered effective band gap energy in the CTC-(1, 2, 4, & 7) NCs [58]. An important optical property of materials is the refractive index (n), which plays a significant role in various applications. The refractive index of the prepared nanoparticles is calculated using the formula [59]:

Table 2 lists the estimated refractive index values, indicating an increase in 'n' with the incorporation of Tb3+ ions and CDs into CeO2. This suggests that both the crystal structure and ion concentration affect the refractive index of these materials, with the refractive index behavior influenced by the degree of Tb3+ ion and CDs incorporation [60].

XPS is employed to analyze the chemical composition and bonding configurations of the CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs. As shown in Fig. 4a, the XPS survey spectrum confirms the presence of C, O, Ce, N, and Tb, consistent with the elemental mapping results for the CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs. The high-resolution Ce 3d spectrum (Fig. 4b) displays two pairs of spin-orbit peaks, indicating the coexistence of Ce(III) and Ce(IV) oxidation states. The two prominent peaks at 885 and 905.5 eV correspond to the 3d3/2 and 3d₅/2 levels, respectively, confirming the presence of CeO2. Peaks labeled as vo, uo, v’, and u’ are associated with Ce3+, whereas peaks v, v’’, v′’’, u, u’’, and u′’’ are attributed to Ce4+ [61]. The observed Ce3+ content is substantial, which serves as evidence for the presence of additional oxygen vacancies in the CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs, resulting in more oxygen defects [62]. The O 1 s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 4c) reveals three distinct peaks at approximately 528.59, 529.71, and 531.07 eV. The peak at 528.59 eV corresponds to lattice oxygen (denoted as OL), the peak at 531.07 eV represents oxygen-containing functional groups linked to C 1 s (C–O), labeled as OC, and the peak at 529.71 eV is attributed to adsorbed water and ions, denoted as Ov [63]. The higher proportion of OL indicates a high level of oxygen vacancies, which enhances photocatalytic activity [64]. Fig. 4d shows the Tb 4d spectrum of the CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs, with a peak at 152.3 eV attributed to the Tb-O-Ce bonding [65]. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4e) shows peaks at 284.4 and 288 eV, corresponding to C–C (sp²) and C–O functional groups, respectively [66]. The N 1 s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 4f) is deconvoluted into two distinct peaks at 399.56 eV and 400.23 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively. Pyrrolic N refers to nitrogen atoms incorporated into a 5 membered ring, where they contribute two p-electrons to the π system. On the other hand, graphitic N represents nitrogen atoms bonded to three adjacent sp² hybridized carbon atoms, contributing electrons to the p-conjugated structure [67].

Fig. 5(a–f) presents FE-SEM images of CeO2:Tb3+ (0–5 mol%) NPs. The images reveal that the NPs exhibit a highly homogeneous and symmetrical spherical morphology. The FE-SEM micrograph of CDs (Fig. 5g) shows a surface morphology with a uniform distribution, characterized by spherical, aggregated nanoparticles at a 1 µm scale. The absence of large agglomerates indicates good dispersion, which is advantageous for applications in sensing, bio-imaging, and photocatalysis. The FE-SEM image of the CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs (Fig. 5h) demonstrates the integration of CDs as smaller, distinct particles adhered to the CeO2:3Tb3+ structure. This successful incorporation of CDs is likely to enhance the optical properties due to synergistic interactions between the CDs and CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs, making the composite suitable for luminescence and photocatalytic applications [68]. Fig. 5(i–n) presents the EDAX spectra of pristine CeO₂ and CeO₂:Tb3+ nanoparticles with varying Tb3+ concentrations (0–5 mol%). All spectra exhibit distinct peaks corresponding to Cerium (Ce), Terbium (Tb), and Oxygen (O), with no evidence of impurity elements, indicating the successful synthesis of CeO₂ through the hydrothermal method. Notably, an increase in Tb3+ concentration results in a corresponding rise in its weight percentage, as observed in the spectra. In contrast, Fig. 5(o) displays the EDAX spectrum of CDs (4 wt%)@CeO₂:3Tb3+ nanocomposites (NCs), revealing the presence of Cerium (Ce), Carbon (C), Terbium (Tb), and Oxygen (O). The detection of Carbon, which is absent in the pure CeO₂ spectra, confirms the effective incorporation of carbon dots (CDs) into the composite. The lack of any extraneous elemental peaks across all spectra further validates the purity of the synthesized materials and the successful fabrication of CDs(4 wt%)@CeO₂:3Tb3+ NCs via the hydrothermal route [69].

TEM analysis further elucidates the morphology of the synthesized samples. Fig. 6a shows the TEM image of CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs, revealing spherical particles with uniform distribution. The particle size distribution, shown in Fig. 6a1, indicates an average particle size of 10.36 nm. Fig. 6b displays the TEM image of CDs, revealing nanoscale dimensions with an average size of 5.06 nm, as depicted in Fig. 6b1. The TEM image of the CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs in Fig. 6c illustrates the successful encapsulation of CDs on the surface of the CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs, with yellow circles highlighting the presence of CDs. The particle size distribution for the NCs, shown in Fig. 6c1, indicates an average size of 5.026 nm, which aligns with the CDs' size shown in Fig. 6b. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs (Fig. 6d) is analysed using Gatan software [70]. The Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), Inverse FFT (IFFT), and line profiles of the crystalline CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs reveal a d-spacing of 0.31 nm, corresponding to the (111) plane. The HR-TEM image of the CDs is shown in Fig. 6e, with a d-spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane, as confirmed by the line profile. Finally, Fig. 6f displays the HR-TEM image of the CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs, showing two planes with d-spacings of 0.31 and 0.21 nm. The line profile in Fig. 6f1 corresponds to the CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs with a d-spacing of 0.31 nm, confirming the presence of CeO2 lattice planes. Fig. 6f2, depicting the FFT pattern and line profile, confirms the graphitic nature of the CDswithin the composite with a d-spacing of 0.21 nm.

The dominant peak at 543 nm corresponds to the green emission, with its intensity increasing up to 3 mol% Tb3+ concentration, followed by a decrease due to concentration quenching (Fig. 7c). This quenching effect occurs primarily because of multi-phonon-assisted non-radiative transitions induced by the higher concentration of Tb3+ ions in the matrix. According to Dexter's theory, this quenching phenomenon can be analyzed by understanding the type of energy transfer interactions between Tb3+ ions [73]. The critical interaction distance (R
c ) between Tb3+ ions within the host matrix is calculated using the following formula:

where k and β are constants, and Q represents the type of multipolar interaction (e.g., Q=3 for nearest-neighbor interactions, Q=6 for dipole-dipole, Q=8 for dipole-quadrupole, and Q=10 for quadrupole-quadrupole interactions). As depicted in Fig. 7d, the plot of log(x) against log(I/x) yields a slope of 1.83, suggesting a Q value of approximately 5.49. This value is closer to 6, indicating that dipole-dipole interactions are predominantly responsible for the observed quenching effect in CeO2:Tb3+ NPs [75]. Incorporating different weight percentages (wt%) of CDs into the CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs significantly enhanced their luminescent properties. Fig. 8a presents the PL excitation spectra of both CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs and CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs. Notably, the excitation spectrum intensity of the CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs increased compared to the original CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs. Moreover, Fig. 8b shows that the emission intensity at 543 nm for CDs@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs is enhanced by approximately 29.2 times compared to CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs. The PL emission spectra for CDs at different concentrations (0, 1, 2, 4, and 7 wt%) combined with CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs are displayed in Figure S3a, while the corresponding emission intensity variation is shown in Figure S3b. The substantial enhancement in PL, with a 29.2-fold increase, can be attributed to FRET between the CDs and the CeO2:3Tb3+ matrix. In FRET, energy is transferred non-radiatively from the excited CDs, acting as the donor, to the nearby Tb3+ ions, serving as the acceptor, within the CeO2 matrix. Fig. 8c illustrates the emission and excitation spectra of CDs, demonstrating that the spectral overlap conditions required for FRET are met. The comparative PL enhancement data of different CDs nanocomposite materials is provided in Table S5 [76–81]. In conclusion, the integration of CDs significantly boosts the PL intensity of CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs, suggesting potential applications in optoelectronic devices.

For FRET to occur effectively, specific conditions must be satisfied. Primarily, the distance between the donor and acceptor molecules must be within a range of approximately 1–10 nm, as FRET efficiency is extremely sensitive to this distance. The efficiency decreases rapidly as the separation between the donor and acceptor exceeds this range [82]. This condition is verified through the FE-SEM (Fig. 5h1) and TEM (Fig. 6c) images of the CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs, which reveal the close adherence of CDs on the surface of CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs, indicating the suitable donor-acceptor proximity. Secondly, there must be a significant overlap between the emission spectrum of the donor (CDs) and the excitation spectrum of the acceptor (CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs), a requirement that is essential for efficient energy transfer via FRET. Fig. 8d confirms the spectral overlap, clearly demonstrating that the emission spectrum of the CDs overlaps with the excitation spectrum of CeO2:3Tb3+, thus fulfilling this condition. Additionally, for FRET to occur efficiently, the transition dipole orientations of the donor and acceptor must be properly aligned. FRET efficiency is maximized when the dipole moments are parallel, contributing to more effective energy transfer, a detail further analyzed in the PL studies of CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs. The FRET mechanism between CDs and CeO2:3Tb3+ can be described as follows: Upon excitation at 356 nm, the CDs absorb photons. Given the sufficient spectral overlap, energy transfer occurs non-radiatively from the excited CDs (acting as the donor) to the nearby Tb3+ ions embedded within the CeO2 matrix (acting as the acceptor) [83]. Once these Tb3+ ions receive energy from the CDs, they relax to their ground state, emitting intense green luminescence at around 543 nm, corresponding to the 5D4 → 7F₅ transition, along with other characteristic transitions depicted in Fig. 8e.

To further understand this energy transfer, decay lifetimes are analyzed. Fig. 9 presents the decay curves for CDs and CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs excited at 318 nm. The decay curve for pure CDs (Fig. 9a) fits a single exponential function [84]:

Analyzing the luminescence decay curves provides strong evidence of FRET between the donor (CDs) and acceptor (CeO2:3Tb3+) molecules. A significant difference is observed when comparing the lifetimes of CDs with and without the presence of the acceptor [85]. The decay curve of CDs without the acceptor (Fig. 9a) shows a single exponential decay with a lifetime of 8.19 ns, indicating no FRET, as the longer lifetime implies the absence of energy transfer. In contrast, the decay curve for CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs (Fig. 9b) shows a much shorter average lifetime of 2.03 ns, suggesting efficient FRET, with the donor's energy being transferred to the acceptor. FRET efficiency (η) can be calculated using the formula:

This indicates that 75.2 % of the donor's energy is transferred non-radiatively to the acceptor, demonstrating high FRET efficiency, signifying that the donor and acceptor are well within the optimal 1–10 nm range for energy transfer. This substantial reduction in the donor’s fluorescence lifetime confirms that a significant portion of the CDs excited-state energy is efficiently transferred to the Tb3+ ions, validating the FRET process between CDs and CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs. Furthermore, the observed enhancement in luminescence for CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs compared to CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs when excited at 386 nm (Figs. 9 c and 9d) further supports the efficient FRET mechanism occurring between the CDs and the Tb3+ ions within the nanocomposite [86].

At an excitation wavelength of 386 nm, the CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs achieved an impressive quantum yield of 94.3 %, substantially higher than the 72.4 % yield for CeO2:3Tb3+ NPs. This value is significantly greater than that reported for other Tb3+ doped phosphors, as detailed in Table S6 [91–95], indicating the superior luminescent properties of CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs.

The synthesized samples demonstrated efficient photocatalytic activity in degrading MB dye under sunlight irradiation. The samples C (CeO2), CT (CeO2:3Tb3+), CTC-1 (CDs(1 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+), CTC-2 (CDs(2 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+), CTC-4 (CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+), and CTC-7 (CDs(7 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+) exhibited enhanced photocatalytic efficiency due to their smaller bandgap, extended light absorption, and improved charge carrier dynamics [96–99]. The degradation mechanism involves the absorption of light by the photocatalyst, which generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) and drives redox reactions. The CDs act as a dopant, introducing new energy levels and reducing the bandgap of the CeO2 host material, thereby enhancing photocatalytic activity. The proposed degradation pathway of the dye molecules, as demonstrated in Fig. 11, can be summarized as follows [100]:

(18) CTC-4 + hν (light) + MB dye → CTC-4 + hν (light) + MB dye*
The photocatalyst absorbs photons, promoting electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), which creates positively charged holes in the VB.

The MB dye molecules undergo oxidative degradation through interactions with ROS, breaking down into smaller, less harmful molecules. Previous studies have reported the photo-degradation of MB using various photocatalysts, and comparative findings are presented in Table S7 [101–106]. The overall efficiency of the photocatalytic process is affected by several factors, including catalyst dosage, solution pH, irradiation time, light source, pollutant concentration, and reaction temperature. This comprehensive analysis demonstrates that the synthesized CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+ NCs exhibit superior photocatalytic activity and potential for applications in environmental remediation and solid-state lighting.

The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of MB is assessed using various catalysts under solar irradiation. As depicted in Fig. 12a, CeO2 alone exhibited a relatively low degradation efficiency, with approximately 49 % MB removal after 120 minutes, indicating negligible photocatalytic activity by pure CeO2. In contrast, the composite nanocatalysts CTC-1 (CDs(1 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+), CTC-2 (CDs(2 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+), CTC-4 (CDs(4 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+), and CTC-7 (CDs(7 wt%)@CeO2:3Tb3+) demonstrated significantly enhanced degradation efficiency compared to the pristine catalyst, as shown in Figure S4.

The enhanced performance of these composites is due to the strong interfacial interaction between the components, which promotes charge separation and transfer, thereby improving photocatalytic activity. The degradation efficiency of the samples is summarized in Fig. 12b, where CTC-4 achieved a remarkable 98 % MB degradation within 120 min, showcasing its superior photocatalytic ability. As the content of CDs increased, the photocatalytic activity is initially enhanced but later declined beyond a certain concentration, likely due to the agglomeration of excess CDs, which can block active sites on the catalyst surface. This accumulation results in a reduced surface area available for catalysis, diminishing the overall photocatalytic efficiency. To further understand the photocatalytic process, the reaction kinetics are analyzed using the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model [107], represented by the following equation:

Here, k represents the apparent reaction rate constant, t denotes the irradiation time, C
t is the concentration of MB at time t, and C
0 is the initial concentration. As shown in Fig. 12c, the photocatalytic degradation process followed a pseudo-first-order kinetic model. The rate constant k values for the different catalysts are provided in Fig. 12d. The CTC-4 composite exhibited the highest k value of 0.031 min−1, which is 5.3 times greater than that of pure CeO2 (0.0058 min−1), indicating the enhanced photocatalytic efficiency of the CTC-4 composite. In contrast, further increasing the CDs content did not lead to a proportional increase in the k value, underscoring the importance of optimizing the CDs concentration. Excessive CDs can serve as recombination centers or hinder the generation of ROS, thereby reducing the photocatalytic efficiency [108]. Additionally, excess CDs may scatter light, reducing light absorption and hindering photocatalytic activity [109]. The degradation efficiencies, k values, and coefficient of determination (R2) for each sample are summarized in Table 4. Given its superior performance, the CTC-4 composite is selected as the most effective photocatalyst for further MB degradation experiments. As shown in Figure S4, the absorbance of MB decreased significantly during the photocatalytic process, affirming the high degradation efficiency of the CTC-4 NCs.

The pH of a solution significantly affects both the rate and efficiency of photocatalytic degradation, as it influences the ionization state of the adsorbate surface. Consequently, the photodegradation process is highly pH-dependent, showing optimal activity within certain pH ranges. To assess this effect, the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye is studied across a pH range of 5–12, using an initial MB concentration of 100 mg/L and 0.5 g/L of the synthesized CTC-4 catalyst. As shown in Fig. 13a, an increase in MB dye decomposition is observed with rising pH levels under visible light exposure for 120 min. The highest removal efficiency (98.1 %) is achieved at pH 12 for CTC-4 NCs, as depicted in Fig. 13a1. The photocatalytic reaction kinetics followed a pseudo-first-order model, as illustrated in Figure S5a. The corresponding rate constant k is also observed to reach its maximum at pH 12, as shown in Fig. 13a2. At higher pH levels, an increase in hydroxyl ions (OH-) leads to the generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and the accumulation of negative charges on the surface of the CTC-4 NCs. This buildup creates strong electrostatic interactions between the catalyst surface and the positively charged MB dye, improving the adsorption and subsequent degradation of the dye particles. This finding aligns with previous reports by Pouretedal and Kadkhodaie [110] and Kalaycıoglu et al. [111], who also identified optimal pH levels of ∼11, 9 and 12 for the maximum degradation of methylene blue.

The amount of photocatalyst introduced is crucial in determining the efficiency of pollutant elimination and the remediation of organic particles in wastewater treatment. To assess the effect of varying photocatalyst (CTC-4) dosages, experiments are conducted using different catalyst masses (0.2–1.2 g/L) under optimal conditions, as shown in Fig. 13b. An increase in catalyst dosage led to more available active sites, resulting in a faster and more efficient decomposition of MB dye. The degradation rate peaked at 0.8 g/L of CTC-4 NCs, achieving a maximum removal efficiency of 97.8 %, as presented in Fig. 13b1. The reaction kinetics fitted well with a pseudo-first-order model (Figure S5b), and the effect of catalyst dosage on the rate constant k is shown in Fig. 13b2. Beyond 0.8 g/L, further increases in catalyst dosage led to a decline in the photodegradation process due to particle aggregation, which increased solution turbidity and limited light penetration [112]. This observation is consistent with previous studies by Zhang et al. [113] and Pouretedal and Kadkhodaie [110], who reported optimal catalyst dosages of 4.0 g/L and 1 g/L, respectively, using CeO2 coated on fly ash cenospheres (FACs) and CeO2 catalysts.

To determine the optimal initial dye concentration, the degradation efficiency of MB dye is examined using different concentrations (20–120 mg/L) while maintaining a fixed pH ∼12 and a catalyst dose of 0.8 g/L (Fig. 13 c). At lower MB concentrations (20 mg/L), a high removal efficiency of 96.5 % is observed, attributed to the availability of more active sites on the catalyst surface relative to the dye molecules. However, as the MB concentration increased, the degradation efficiency decreased, reaching about 14 % at 120 mg/L. The kinetic study, fitting the pseudo-first-order model, is shown in Figure S5c, and the corresponding effect on k is illustrated in Figure 13c
2 .

Higher dye concentrations result in reduced light penetration into the solution and increased competition for active sites on the photocatalyst, both of which hinder the overall degradation efficiency. These results are consistent with findings by Murugan et al. [114] and El-Katori et al. [115], who reported optimal MB concentrations of 10 mg/L and 1 mg/L using CeO2 and CdS/SnO2 nanoparticles, respectively. To evaluate the practical applicability and durability of CTC-4 NCs for real-world effluent treatment, cyclic photocatalysis experiments are conducted to assess its stability over multiple degradation cycles. The photocatalytic efficiency is evaluated over five consecutive cycles, during which the catalyst particles are recovered, washed with water and ethanol, and then dried after each cycle. The C/C₀ curve and degradation efficiency for each cycle are illustrated in Figs. 13 a and 13 b, respectively. The results demonstrate consistent degradation efficiency, with only a slight decline of around 0.6 % after the fifth cycle, indicating that CTC-4 NCs maintain their catalytic activity and structural integrity over repeated use. The XRD pattern of CTC-4 NCs after five cycles (Figure S6a) remained similar to the original, with no significant structural phase changes, suggesting the catalyst's robustness. However, a minor shift in peak intensity is noted, indicating the absence of impurities from dye molecules. The FTIR spectrum (Figure S6b) also showed that the peak at 1036 cm−1, attributed to C–O stretching vibrations, became stronger and broader due to the formation of organic byproducts from the degraded dye solutions [116]. Overall, the XRD and FTIR analyses confirmed that CTC-4 NCs exhibit excellent stability and recyclability, retaining their structural and chemical properties even after multiple photocatalytic cycles. The multifunctional properties of CeO2 NPs, including their antibacterial and antimicrobial capabilities, suggest potential applications in healthcare and water sanitation, as they effectively combat microorganisms in polluted water bodies [117]. Additionally, CeO2 NPs show potential in a variety of health-related applications, including cancer treatment, antioxidant therapy, wound healing, drug delivery, and clinical activities. These properties make CeO2 NPs more versatile and stable compared to other metal and rare earth oxide nanoparticles, such as SnO2, NiO, CuO, Eu2O3, and Ru2O3 [118].

To assess the impact of photocatalytically treated water on seed germination, water samples obtained after treating MB dye with the CTC-4 catalyst are used to germinate chickpea seeds. For comparison, untreated MB dye solution (at 100 ppm) and distilled water are used as controls. The results, presented in Table 5, indicate that seed germination began on day 2 (within 24–36 h) in all treatments except for the MB dye solution, where no germination is observed. Notably, seeds germinated fastest in the CTC-4 treated water, with an average germination time of 26 hours, surpassing other treatments ( Fig. 14). In contrast, the control (distilled water) took about 30 hours for the seeds to sprout. Furthermore, the CTC-4 treated seeds achieved a 100 % germination rate, outperforming CeO2:3Tb3+ (CT) at 90 %, CeO2 (C) at 80 %, and the control at 70 %. In comparison, the CeO2 NPs showed a lower germination rate of 65 % when exposed to the MB dye solution, with a mean germination time ranging between 13.25 and 14.50 h.

Measurements taken after harvest further highlighted improved seedling phenotype across CDs-treated groups. Table 5a shows that both shoot and root lengths increased for CTC-4 treated groups, peaking at 4.13 ± 1.21 cm and 2.82 ± 1.08 cm respectively on Day 7. Shoot and root diameters followed a similar trend (Table 5b). For CTC-4 treated groups, shoot and root diameters reached 2.72 ± 1.04 mm and 1.67 ± 0.52 mm, respectively substantially greater than the control (2.35 ± 0.95 mm and 1.42 ± 0.32 mm). Root branching, detailed in Table 5c, is also enhanced. At Day 5, root branches in the CTC-4 treated group totalled 16, compared to 12 in the control. By Day 7, this increased to 20 for CTC-4 group, which is higher than the control (17 branches) as seen in Fig. 15a.

Calculations revealed that the Plant Inhibition Growth (PIG) value is highest for MB dye (100 ppm), confirming its strong toxicity to plant growth, as evidenced by 0 % germination and 0 vigor index I. In contrast, the CeO₂ treated group exhibited a PIG value of 0.3, suggesting moderate phytotoxicity [119,120], while CeO₂:3Tb3+ showed a slightly negative PIG value (-0.1), indicating minimal inhibition. For the CDs (4 wt%)@CeO₂:3Tb3+-treated group, the PIG value is −0.2, which is negligible and indicative of the non-toxic, biocompatible nature of the CDs in the degraded MB dye water (Fig. 15b). Furthermore, Vigor Index I, derived from seedling root length, reached its maximum value of 695 for the CTC-4 treated group, significantly higher than the control (535) and other treatments, indicating optimal seedling vigor (Fig. 15c). The enhancement in vigor suggests improved early development, which is critical for long-term plant health and productivity. In Fig. 15d, the germination index showed a steady increase with increasing CDs concentrations, consistent with previous results and affirming the growth-promoting role of CDs in nano-priming. This aligns with the highest germination index (129.9) and shortest mean germination time (24.0 days) among all tested groups, emphasizing the efficacy of CDs in supporting rapid and healthy seed germination (Table 5d). Notably, 100 % germination is observed across all treated groups except MB dye, where no germination occurred, confirming the inhibitory nature of untreated dye-contaminated water. The control group, despite 100 % germination, had a longer mean germination time (26.4 days) and a lower germination index (100.0) than the CTC-4 treated group. These values clearly demonstrate the superior priming potential of CTC-4 in degraded water systems. In summary, the CDs(4 wt%)@CeO₂:3Tb3+ NCs not only mitigates the toxicity of MB dye but also promotes seed germination, accelerates emergence, and enhances seedling Vigor, validating its potential for agricultural applications in dye-contaminated environments.

The larger surface area of CDs facilitates their interaction with seed biochemistry, triggering the germination process and promoting seedling growth [121]. These findings align with previous research, where Pooja Chauhan et al. demonstrated that coating chickpea seeds with CDs improved water absorption, enhancing the seed germination rate and ensuring better establishment of seedlings [122]. Various studies have employed metal oxide nanoparticles, such as tin oxide, zinc, titanium, iron, gold, silver, and copper, to enhance seed priming a process that breaks seed dormancy across different crops. These NPs induce an increase in ROS within seeds, promoting rapid germination [123]. The dosage and concentration of synthesized NCs, along with the duration of seed priming, are crucial factors influencing seed germination and subsequent seedling development.

MB from industrial waste poses significant environmental hazards. Exposure to MB can cause health problems in humans, including skin irritation, respiratory issues, and potentially carcinogenic effects. In aquatic environments, MB interferes with photosynthesis in aquatic plants by blocking sunlight, resulting in oxygen depletion and negatively affecting fish and other aquatic organisms. Additionally, the dye can inhibit plant growth and cause toxicity, resulting in stunted growth and even death of trees and plants. These impacts highlight the need for effective wastewater treatment to mitigate the harmful effects of methylene blue on the environment. The schematic representation of effect of methylene blue on environment is given in Fig. 16a. The increasing discharge of dye-laden effluents into aquatic ecosystems from various industries poses significant environmental and health hazards, leading to issues such as genetic mutations, skin allergies, liver damage, and gastrointestinal disorders [124]. The rising concerns about dye pollution have urged researchers to develop advanced techniques for removing harmful dyes from wastewater. Fig. 16b illustrates the proposed mechanism of using MB dye-degraded water for agricultural applications. NCs, like the synthesized CTC-4, have garnered attention due to their larger surface area and enhanced photocatalytic properties, making them effective for wastewater treatment [125]. Their non-toxic nature, minimal residual effects compared to chemical treatments, cost-effectiveness, and sustainability make them suitable for such applications, especially when synthesized from bioresources like plant materials. CDs have emerged as promising fluorescent nanomaterials with potential applications in agriculture, particularly for enhancing seed germination. These carbon-based nanoparticles, typically less than 10 nm in diameter, possess adjustable surface properties that make them valuable for biological applications. CeO2 NPs have also been studied for their potential to enhance seed germination and plant growth, owing to their unique redox properties. CeO2 NPs can act as both antioxidants and pro-oxidants depending on environmental conditions, thereby influencing physiological and biochemical processes during germination [126]. Combining CeO2 and CDs offers synergistic advantages for seed germination. CeO2 nanoparticles provide antioxidant properties and enhance enzyme activity, while CDs facilitate water absorption, nutrient delivery, and efficient light utilization. Together, they accelerate germination and promote healthier, more robust early seedling growth. In this study, the photocatalytic efficiency of the synthesized CTC-4 NCs is examined for its potential application in seed germination. The phytotoxicity of the CeO2:3Tb3+ nanocomposite is also assessed in chickpea seeds when combined with CDs. The results indicate that the CTC-4 nanocomposite effectively enhanced germination and early seedling growth, demonstrating its potential for sustainable agricultural applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Successfully synthesized CDs@CeO₂:3Tb3+ NCs using a hydrothermal method with coffee grounds as a sustainable carbon source.
Demonstrated significant photocatalytic efficiency for detoxifying MB dye, a hazardous industrial pollutant.
Achieved a remarkable 29.2-fold enhancement in PL intensity, attributed to an efficient FRET mechanism with a transfer efficiency of 75 %.
Exhibited green emission at 543 nm with high CP of 98 % and CCT of 4937 K, suitable for cool LED lighting applications.
Attained 98 % MB dye degradation efficiency under visible light, outperforming CeO₂ and CeO₂:3Tb3+ NCs.
Confirmed the near-complete detoxification of the treated effluent through phytotoxicity tests using Cicer arietinum seeds, showing efficient germination in treated water.

TC-4 treated groups showed improved shoot and root lengths (4.13 ± 1.21 cm and 2.82 ± 1.08 cm) and diameters (2.72 ± 1.04 mm and 1.67 ± 0.52 mm) by Day 7. Root branches in the CTC-4 group rose from 16 on Day 5–20 on Day 7, surpassing control values (12 and 17).
MB dye caused 0 % germination with a high PIG value of 100. CDs (4 wt%)@CeO₂:3Tb3+ showed negligible phytotoxicity (PIG −0.2), confirming its safety.
CTC-4 achieved the highest Vigor Index I (695) and germination index (129.9), with a shortest mean germination time (24.0 days). 100 % germination is observed in all treated groups except MB dye.
